APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses reading comprehension/question answering with a novel gated attention and self-matching architecture and is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. It uses standard methods and evaluation (pointer networks, EM/F1 on SQuAD) and reports strong results (single-model EM 71.3% hidden test, ensemble 75.9%), comparing against established baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.). The problem formulation, datasets, metrics, and thorough related-work coverage match community expectations, and the writing quality is good, indicating clear communication to the target audience. | B(5): This paper addresses reading comprehension / QA — a central topic for top NLP/ML venues — by proposing gated attention and gated self-matching networks and using pointer networks to predict answer spans. It evaluates on SQuAD with standard EM/F1 metrics and compares against relevant baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.), reporting strong results (71.3 EM single, 75.9 ensemble). The problem formulation, methods, and evaluation follow community norms, and related work coverage is thorough, so the paper is well positioned for a top conference audience.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses reading comprehension/question answering with a novel gated attention and self-matching architecture and is squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML conferences. It uses standard methods and evaluation (pointer networks, EM/F1 on SQuAD) and reports strong results (single-model EM 71.3% hidden test, ensemble 75.9%), comparing against established baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.). The problem formulation, datasets, metrics, and thorough related-work coverage match community expectations, and the writing quality is good, indicating clear communication to the target audience.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses reading comprehension / QA — a central topic for top NLP/ML venues — by proposing gated attention and gated self-matching networks and using pointer networks to predict answer spans. It evaluates on SQuAD with standard EM/F1 metrics and compares against relevant baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, DCN, etc.), reporting strong results (71.3 EM single, 75.9 ensemble). The problem formulation, methods, and evaluation follow community norms, and related work coverage is thorough, so the paper is well positioned for a top conference audience.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important problem (machine reading comprehension) and introduces concrete architectural ideas — a gated attention mechanism and gated self-matching attention to aggregate evidence across passages — that achieved strong SQuAD results (single-model EM 71.3% on the hidden test, ensemble 75.9%), outperforming many contemporaneous baselines. These mechanisms are broadly applicable to sequence-pair and span-prediction tasks, so the work is likely to influence subsequent model design and be built upon, though the evaluation is currently limited to SQuAD and some attention weights remain diffused as noted, which suggests impactful but not transformative influence. | B(4): The paper addresses an important problem (reading comprehension/QA) and introduces a concrete architectural contribution—gated attention plus gated self-matching attention to aggregate evidence across a passage—which achieved strong results on SQuAD (single-model EM 71.3% hidden test, ensemble 75.9%). Those empirical gains over competitive baselines (e.g., BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention) make it likely researchers will adopt and build on the self-matching and gating ideas. Broader impact is somewhat constrained by evaluation on SQuAD only, so while the work is likely to strongly influence the QA subfield and model design, its transformative effect beyond this area is limited until demonstrated on other tasks/datasets.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims (gated attention and gated self-matching mechanisms, plus pointer-network answer prediction) are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence on SQuAD (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) with comparisons to appropriate baselines. The summary does not report mathematical derivations or proofs, so I cannot verify their completeness, but the described architecture is plausible. Experimental methodology is largely sound — relevant baselines, EM/F1 metrics, and hyperparameter reporting — but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and cross-dataset validation. Overall the conclusions are supported by the reported results, with only minor concerns about reproducibility and broader generalization left unaddressed. | B(4): The paper's main claims (gated attention + gated self-matching + pointer-network output) are clearly instantiated and supported by empirical results on SQuAD (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) and comparison to appropriate baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, Coattention, etc.). Mathematical detail is not highlighted in the summary but nothing indicates incorrect derivations; hyperparameters are reported. Experimental methodology is broadly sound with strong baselines, though the summary does not mention statistical significance tests or evaluation beyond SQuAD, and the authors acknowledge limited generalization analysis, which are minor gaps that do not undermine the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims (gated attention and gated self-matching mechanisms, plus pointer-network answer prediction) are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence on SQuAD (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) with comparisons to appropriate baselines. The summary does not report mathematical derivations or proofs, so I cannot verify their completeness, but the described architecture is plausible. Experimental methodology is largely sound — relevant baselines, EM/F1 metrics, and hyperparameter reporting — but the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and cross-dataset validation. Overall the conclusions are supported by the reported results, with only minor concerns about reproducibility and broader generalization left unaddressed.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims (gated attention + gated self-matching + pointer-network output) are clearly instantiated and supported by empirical results on SQuAD (single-model EM 71.3%, ensemble 75.9%) and comparison to appropriate baselines (BiDAF, Match-LSTM, Coattention, etc.). Mathematical detail is not highlighted in the summary but nothing indicates incorrect derivations; hyperparameters are reported. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents solid experimental evidence on SQuAD, reporting strong single-model (EM 71.3%) and ensemble (75.9%) results and comparing against a wide set of baselines (BiDAF, Match‑LSTM, DCN, etc.), and it reports hyperparameters and provides thorough related-work coverage. It does, however, evaluate only on SQuAD and does not explicitly report ablation or sensitivity analyses in the provided summary, which leaves some questions about which components drive gains. The authors do discuss some failure modes (self-matching attention sometimes diffuse) and note dataset/generalization limits, but lack of code availability is not mentioned, so complete reproducibility is unclear. | B(3): The paper presents strong empirical results on SQuAD with comparisons to many baselines and reports hyperparameters, but evaluation is limited to a single dataset which weakens generality. Ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are not mentioned in the summary, so crucial component-level validation appears lacking. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors discuss some failure cases and dataset limitations, but code is not mentioned so full reproducibility is only partially supported.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents solid experimental evidence on SQuAD, reporting strong single-model (EM 71.3%) and ensemble (75.9%) results and comparing against a wide set of baselines (BiDAF, Match‑LSTM, DCN, etc.), and it reports hyperparameters and provides thorough related-work coverage. It does, however, evaluate only on SQuAD and does not explicitly report ablation or sensitivity analyses in the provided summary, which leaves some questions about which components drive gains. The authors do discuss some failure modes (self-matching attention sometimes diffuse) and note dataset/generalization limits, but lack of code availability is not mentioned, so complete reproducibility is unclear.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents strong empirical results on SQuAD with comparisons to many baselines and reports hyperparameters, but evaluation is limited to a single dataset which weakens generality. Ablation studies or sensitivity analyses are not mentioned in the summary, so crucial component-level validation appears lacking. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the authors discuss some failure cases and dataset limitations, but code is not mentioned so full reproducibility is only partially supported.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper proposes a focused architectural extension—adding an extra gate to attention-based recurrent networks and a gated self-matching attention layer to aggregate passage-wide evidence—which is clearly differentiated from prior QA models (e.g., Match-LSTM, BiDAF, coattention models). These techniques represent a non-trivial, targeted improvement over existing attention+pointer architectures rather than a wholly new problem formulation or paradigm. Given the reliance on established components (attention, recurrent encoders, pointer networks) and the incremental nature of combining gating with self-matching, the contribution is moderately novel rather than highly original. | B(4): The paper proposes a clear, novel architectural addition (an extra gate on attention-based recurrent networks) and introduces a gated self-matching attention mechanism to aggregate evidence across the whole passage—both contributions are explicitly differentiated from prior attention- and pointer-based QA models (e.g., Match-LSTM, BiDAF, Ans-Ptr). While the use of pointer networks for span prediction is standard, the gated self-matching idea and the gate to modulate passage-word importance constitute non-trivial extensions rather than mere combinations of existing pieces. Given the strong SQuAD results reported, these changes would be informative to experts but are best characterized as notable rather than groundbreaking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a focused architectural extension—adding an extra gate to attention-based recurrent networks and a gated self-matching attention layer to aggregate passage-wide evidence—which is clearly differentiated from prior QA models (e.g., Match-LSTM, BiDAF, coattention models). CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The summary indicates a clear, logical organization (intro -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) with the main contributions stated early (gated attention and gated self-matching networks) and a well-described methodology applied to SQuAD. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage as thorough; experimental presentation appears informative (baseline comparisons, EM/F1 metrics, and hyperparameters reported). Notation consistency is not specified in the summary and the paper notes some limitations (attention distribution and single-dataset evaluation), so I rate presentation as strong overall with minor presentation/details gaps. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from method (gated attention, gated self-matching, pointer networks) to experiments (SQuAD results, EM/F1 comparisons). The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good, with thorough related-work coverage. Notation and experimental details seem well-presented (hyperparameters reported), though the summary does not mention figures/tables or code availability, indicating minor presentation gaps.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of appropriate, recent baselines (e.g., Match-LSTM, BiDAF, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR, Multi-Perspective Matching, Fine-Grained Gating) and reports results on the same dataset (SQuAD) using standard metrics (EM and F1). Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper clearly describes how its gated attention and self-matching components differ from prior approaches. However, the summary does not explicitly state whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or details every experimental condition (e.g., ensemble vs single-model differences), so there are minor omissions in transparency of comparisons. | B(4): The paper compares against a comprehensive set of strong, recent SQuAD baselines (e.g., BiDAF, Match-LSTM+Ans-Ptr, Dynamic Coattention, RaSoR, etc.) and reports standard metrics (EM and F1) on the same dataset, so comparisons appear appropriate and fair. Related work coverage is described as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, and the proposed model’s SQuAD results (single-model EM 71.3, ensemble 75.9) are directly comparable to the listed baselines. The summary does not explicitly state whether the authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably or better or fully detail matched training/ensembling conditions, so there is a minor omission in clarity about those points.

main_claims: Propose a gated attention-based recurrent network that adds an additional gate to attention-based recurrent networks to modulate passage-word importance with respect to the question.
Introduce a gated self-matching network that applies self-matching attention to aggregate evidence from the whole passage and refine passage representations.

Use pointer networks on top of the refined passage representation to predict start and end positions of answer spans, yielding strong performance on SQuAD (single model EM 71.3% on hidden test, ensemble 75.9%).
_summary.methodology: Match question and passage with gated attention-based recurrent networks to obtain question-aware passage representations, apply self-matching attention to aggregate evidence across the passage and refine representations, then use pointer networks to predict answer span boundaries.
limitations: For some words, the self-matching attention weights are more evenly distributed between evidence and irrelevant parts (i.e., not always focused on key evidence).
Model evaluation is conducted on SQuAD; applying gated self-matching networks to other datasets is left as future work.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - reading comprehension and question answering
